text.fullText: From: mkearns525@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/10/2026 8:35:23 AM Subject: Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes -------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from mkearns525@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water will have a negative effect on the environment. Clean water is critical for life for all of us and once it is polluted, it can’t be unpolluted. Congress enacted the Clean Water Act to protect, restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. The EPA’s proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and just plain wrong. Specifically: 1. ) I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations. 2. ) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. 3. ) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water. 4. ) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 5. ) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish. Thank you for considering my comments, Sincerely, Ma Meg Kearns 525 Sparkman Ave Duluth, MN 55803-2149 mkearns525@gmail.com
